coredata.dc:title: Synthesis dielectric and mechanical properties of paddy straw derived graphene quantum dots-stone waste nanocomposite
coredata.dc:creator: Kumar Chaturvedi, Ashish
Pappu, Asokan
Kumar Srivastava, Avanish
Kumar Gupta, Manoj
coredata.dc:description: In present work, graphene quantum dots (GQDs) was synthesized from paddy straw using the cost effective hydrothermal method. High strength and moisture resistant GQDs reinforced stone waste particulates nanocomposites were prepared for the first time. Structural, morphological and functional group were analyzed using X-Ray diffraction, SEM and FT-IR techniques, respectively. Interestingly, GQDs-stone nanocomposites exhibited low dielectric constant (ɛ′ ~ 10) as compared to pristine stone composites (ɛ′ ~ 55). High flexural strength up to 60 MPa was achieved from GQDs reinforcement in stone waste nanocomposites. The high flexural strength and low dielectric constant from GQDs-stone waste polymer composite sheet were attributed due to high mechanical strength and sp2 hybridized covalent bond of GQDs and its high conductivity.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon materials
Graphene
Nanocomposites
Electrical properties
Mechanical properties
Waste materials

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Synthesis process of graphene quantum dots (GQDs) from paddy straw and GQDs reinforced stone nanocomposites, (b) FT-IR spectrum of GQDs and (c) GQDs reinforced stone waste nanocomposites.
(a) XRD pattern of GQDs (b) FE-SEM of stone waste, (c) low and (d) high magnified FE-SEM image of GQDs-stone nanocomposites.

Variation of (a) dielectric constant (b) dissipation factor (c) ac conductivity with frequencies for stone waste composites and GQDs-stone waste nanocomposites (d) flexural strength of pristine and GQDs-stone nanocomposites.

head.ce:title: Synthesis dielectric and mechanical properties of paddy straw derived graphene quantum dots-stone waste nanocomposite

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: CSIR, Advanced Materials and Processes Research Institute, Madhya Pradesh, 462026, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Academy of Scientific and Innovative Research (AcSIR)

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Academy of Scientific and Innovative Research (AcSIR), Ghaziabad 201002, India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In present work, graphene quantum dots (GQDs) was synthesized from paddy straw using the cost effective hydrothermal method. High strength and moisture resistant GQDs reinforced stone waste particulates nanocomposites were prepared for the first time. Structural, morphological and functional group were analyzed using X-Ray diffraction, SEM and FT-IR techniques, respectively. Interestingly, GQDs-stone nanocomposites exhibited low dielectric constant (ɛ′ ~ 10) as compared to pristine stone composites (ɛ′ ~ 55). High flexural strength up to 60 MPa was achieved from GQDs reinforcement in stone waste nanocomposites. The high flexural strength and low dielectric constant from GQDs-stone waste polymer composite sheet were attributed due to high mechanical strength and sp2 hybridized covalent bond of GQDs and its high conductivity.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon nanostructures have gained tremendous attraction due to their outstanding mechanical strength, electrical conductivity, chemical and thermal properties [1,2]. Particularly, graphene/graphene quantum dots (GQDs) have been recently utilized as active materials in various applications including solar cell, optoelectronic devices, flexible display devices, drug delivery and biosensors [3,4]. GQD has only one atomic layer of carbon atoms and due to smaller/nanoscale size, GQDs has a better prospect over graphene/graphene oxide. Moreover, GQDs can be used as filler to fabricate the smart nanocomposites with enhanced mechanical properties due to its outstanding mechanical stiffness [5,6]. Agrowaste such as paddy straw of about 720 million tons (MT) are produced yearly but little amount was utilized for any purpose and huge amount are burnet in open atmosphere that causes air pollution [7]. Similarly, stoneis often used for the construction of building houses. However, about 50 MT of stone waste are generated every year and no proper methods for disposal is still available. Various methods such as chemical vapor deposition (CVD) and physical vapor deposition (PVD), have been reported to prepare the GQDs [8]. However, to the best of our knowledge, synthesis of GQDs from agrowaste and fabrication of GQDs with stones in epoxy polymer are not reported yet. These nanocomposites have great potential in numerous applications in high performance building/flame retardant materials, electrical insulation, flexible nanoelectronics, automobile body parts, electronic packaging fields, and in self-healing devices. Moreover, improving mechanical and electrical properties of nanocomposite with addition of GQDs/industrial waste is challenging and desirable. In the present work, paddy straw was used to prepare the GQDs by hydrothermal route. Hybrid nanocomposites of pristine stone waste and GQDs reinforced stone nanocomposites are prepared. Effect of GQDs on flexural strength and dielectric property were analyzed for the first time.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of GQDs from paddy straw
ce:section.ce:para: Initially, 2.0 g of paddy straw was taken and fine powder was obtained by mechanically grinding. The powder was then washed with 0.15 M HCl and heated at 50 °C for 3 h. To synthesize the GQDs, 100 mg of processed paddy straw powder was dissolved in 25 ml of water and kept into Teflon lined autoclave at 160 °C for 6 hr. After natural cooling, solution has been filtered, and centrifuged at 27,00 rpm for 20 min to get GQDs. The detailed synthesis procedure is illustrated in the Fig. 1 . The fabrication process of GQDs-reinforced stone waste polymer nanocomposites is given in Supporting Information.

X-ray diffraction (XRD) (Rigaku Mini Flex II) was used to investigate the structural property. Fourier-transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR) spectra was recorded in the range of 500–4000 cm−1 using Thermal Scientific Nicolet iS50. Feld emission scanning electron microscope (FE-SEM) was performed to investigates the surface morphology of GQDs, stone waste and nanocomposites. Dielectric property was measured using Keysight LCR meter (E4980A). Flexural strength of the sample was measured using ASTM D790-17 and LRX plus Universal testing machine (Lloyd Instruments, UK).

Synthesis of GQDs from paddy straw and preparation of GQDs-stone based nanocomposites are illustrated in the Fig. 1. The original image of the GQDs-stone nanocomposites is shown in the left bottom side of Fig. 1a. FT-IR spectrum of GQDs and GQDs-stone waste nanocomposites are shown in Fig. 1b and c, respectively. The absorption band appeared at 1610 cm−1 is attributed to aromatic bending of CC bonds and CO stretches. Peak observed at 3360 cm−1 reveals OH stretching of GQDs (Fig. 1b). The spectral peaks at 1245 cm−1 and 1030 cm−1 indicate the presence of functional group of CH and CO, respectively [9,10]. The FT-IR spectrum of GQDs-stone waste nanocomposites is shown in Fig. 1c. The peaks observed at 1507 cm−1 and 1244 cm−1 are attributed due to c-c stretching vibration in aromatic and asymmetrical aromatic C-O stretch and aliphatic CO stretch [11]. The absorption peak at 830.69 cm−1 is corresponds to –CH out of plane deformation in aromatic epoxy resin [11]. The signature peaks of GQDs are also observed in the FT-IR spectrum of GQDs-stone nanocomposites.

The XRD pattern of as grown GQDs is shown in Fig. 2 a. XRD spectrum shows an intense prominent diffraction peak at 25.31° corresponds to well known (002) plane which suggests the formation of crystalline GQDs [12]. GQDs prepared from paddy straw also exhibit a characteristic blue-green light emission under excitation wavelength of 254 nm (top right side in Fig. 1a). Fig. 2b depicts the FE-SEM image of stone waste, showing irregular morphologies with size of 1–10 µm. The distribution of GQDs in stone nanocomposites is shown in Fig. 2c and d. A uniform distribution of GQDs within stone-epoxy matrix can be clearly seen. High-Resolution TEM image of GQDs, confirms the size of GQDs in range of 5–15 nm (Fig. S1) and FE-SEM images indicate the strong interfacial bonding of GQDs with epoxy-stone system (Fig. 2d).

Dielectric properties of pristine and GQDs reinforced stone nanocomposites are measured in frequency range of 20 Hz −2 MHz at RT. The dielectric constant (ε’) of nanocomposites was obtained by using the below relation [13]
(1)
ε
'
=
t ×
C P
ε 0
× A
where ε0, ε’’, Cp are permittivity of free space, imaginary part of dielectric constant, capacitance and A and t are area and thickness of the sample, respectively. Variation of dielectric constant (ε
'
) for pristine and GQDs reinforced stone nanocomposites are shown in Fig. 3 a. In both samples, dielectric constant decreases with increase of frequency due to the contributions of dipolar, electronic, ionic polarization and space charge polarizations. Stone waste nanocomposites exhibited dielectric constant of 55, whereas dramatically GQDs-stone nanocomposite shows ultra-low dielectric constant of 10 at same frequency. Dielectric constant of pristine epoxy was found to be about 5 at same frequency (Fig. S2). It was proposed that the nano-scale size and conducting nature of GQDs introduce many interfaces and accumulation of GQDs at epoxy-stones interfaces may restricts the dipolar polarization at interface regions which may also block the orientation movement of epoxy chain which results drastic reduction in dielectric constant was observed [14]. Very low dissipation factor (tan δ) of 0.02 for stone composites and slightly higher tanδ of 0.05 were obtained in case of GQDs-stone nanocomposites (Fig. 3b). AC conductivity (σ) of both the samples are also calculated using formula [14]
(2)
σ = 2 π f
tan
δ
∈ r
ε 0
where f is applied frequency. AC conductivity of samples increases with increase in frequency due to the dispersive nature of conductivity and it follows the Josher power law relation σ = Aωs, where A is constant and the exponent s is a frequency (ω)-dependent parameter having values less than unity. It is worth to point out that GQDs-stone nanocomposite exhibits a high ac conductivity of (~4.49 × 10−11 Ω−1 cm−1) as compared to pristine stone composites (~2.3 × 10-11 Ω−1 cm−1) at 200 Hz. The increase in AC conductivity for GQDs reinforced nanocomposites can be explained on the basis of the high conductivity of GQDs compared to stone waste particulates (Fig. 3c). The increase in AC conductivity with frequencies is attributed to the pumping force of the applied frequency that helps in transferring the charge carriers between the different localized states in stone-epoxy matrix as well liberating the trapped charges from the different trapping centres in GQDs and stone nanocomposites samples.

To study the effect of GQDs on mechanical property of stone nanocomposite, flexural strength (σ) of pristine and GQDs-stone nanocomposites were measured [15]. The flexural strength-strain curves of both samples are shown in Fig. 3d. Pristine stone composite shows flexural strength of 40 MPa, whereas GQDs reinforced stone nanocomposites exhibited a very flexural strength of 60 MPa. The flexural strength of pristine epoxy was about 33 MPa (Fig. S3). This remarkable increase in flexural strength is due to the strong interfacial/interchange network bonding of GQDs with stone waste particulates and epoxy resin and fine dispersion of GQDs. The high flexural strength is also attributed to the high mechanical stiffness of sp2 hybridized covalent bond of GQDs and formation of minimum voids/cavitation’s in GQDs-stone nanocomposites under polar DMF solvent. The performance comparison of GQDs-stone nanocomposites with similar reported composites is also discussed in Supporting Information.

In summary, crystalline graphene quantum dots (GQDs) was grown by agrowastes (paddy straw) via hydrothermal route. Pristine stone waste polymer composite and GQDs reinforced stone nanocomposites were developed for the first time using compressive molding techniques. GQDs reinforced stone polymer nanocomposites exhibited very low dielectric constant of 10 compared to high dielectric of stone composite of 55. Very high flexural strength up to 60 MPa was achieved from the GQDs- reinforced stone waste based nanocomposites. The findings will be pertinent to the environmental friendly synthesis of GQDs and fabrication of waste materials based smart nanocomposites for multifunctional applications.

Ashish Kumar Chaturvedi: Conceptualization, Methodology, Writing - original draft. Asokan Pappu: Conceptualization, Supervision. Avanish Kumar Srivastava: Supervision, Writing - review & editing. Manoj Kumar Gupta: Conceptualization, Supervision, Data curation, Writing - review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Authors are very thankful to Dr. A. K. Srivastva, Director, CSIR-AMPRI, Bhopal for giving permission to publish the work. M. K Gupta is greatful to CSIR, India for granting MLP -116 and MLP-115 projects. Mr. Ashish is thankful to AICTE for fellowship.
